questions.stem: For the scalar model $\dot{x} = f(x)$, an equilibrium (steady state) $x^*$ is a value satisfying
questions.explanation: At an equilibrium the state does not change, so $\dot{x} = 0$, that is $f(x^*) = 0$. The condition $f'(x^*) = 0$ concerns the stability or a bifurcation, and $f(x^*) = x^*$ would be a fixed point of a map, not of the flow.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For $\dot{x} = f(x)$, the equilibrium $x^*$ is linearly stable when
questions.explanation: Writing $x = x^* + \eta$, the perturbation obeys $\dot{\eta} \approx f'(x^*)\eta$, so $\eta(t) = \eta_0 e^{f'(x^*)t}$. It decays (stability) precisely when $f'(x^*) < 0$. A positive slope gives growth, and a zero slope is the marginal case.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Consider $\dot{x} = x(1 - x)$. The equilibria and their stability are
questions.explanation: Roots of $x(1-x) = 0$ are $x^* = 0$ and $x^* = 1$. With $f'(x) = 1 - 2x$: $f'(0) = 1 > 0$ (unstable) and $f'(1) = 1 - 2 = -1 < 0$ (stable). This is the logistic pattern.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For $\dot{x} = x - x^3$, classify the three equilibria.
questions.explanation: Factor $x - x^3 = x(1 - x^2) = 0$, giving $x^* = 0, \pm 1$. With $f'(x) = 1 - 3x^2$: $f'(0) = 1 > 0$ (unstable), and $f'(\pm 1) = 1 - 3 = -2 < 0$ (stable). The outer two states are stable, the middle one unstable.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For a planar system $\dot{x} = f(x,y)$, $\dot{y} = g(x,y)$, the Jacobian matrix is
questions.explanation: The Jacobian collects the first partial derivatives: row one is the gradient of $f$, row two the gradient of $g$, i.e. $J = \begin{pmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{pmatrix}$. Evaluated at an equilibrium it governs the linearized dynamics.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For a $2 \times 2$ Jacobian $J$ with trace $\tau$ and determinant $\Delta$, the eigenvalues satisfy
questions.explanation: The characteristic equation of a $2\times 2$ matrix is $\lambda^2 - (\text{tr}\,J)\lambda + \det J = 0$, that is $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$. Its roots are $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}\right)$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An equilibrium of a planar system is asymptotically stable (both eigenvalues have negative real part) if and only if
questions.explanation: For the quadratic $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$, the sum of roots is $\tau$ and the product is $\Delta$. Both roots have negative real part exactly when $\tau < 0$ (negative sum) and $\Delta > 0$ (positive product). This is the trace-determinant stability criterion.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: If the determinant of the Jacobian at an equilibrium is negative ($\Delta < 0$), the equilibrium is
questions.explanation: When $\Delta < 0$ the product of the eigenvalues is negative, so they are real with opposite signs. One direction grows, the other decays: this is a saddle point, which is always unstable.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Find the eigenvalues and type of the equilibrium for the linear system with Jacobian $J = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The matrix is upper triangular, so the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $\lambda = -2$ and $\lambda = -3$. Both are real and negative, so the equilibrium is a stable node. (Check: $\tau = -5 < 0$, $\Delta = 6 > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0$.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the system with Jacobian $J = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, the equilibrium is
questions.explanation: Here $\tau = 2$ and $\Delta = (1)(1) - (2)(2) = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0$. A negative determinant means a saddle. (Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}(2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}) = 1 \pm 2$, i.e. $3$ and $-1$, confirming opposite signs.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For $J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -4 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, the eigenvalues and equilibrium type are
questions.explanation: $\tau = 0$ and $\Delta = (0)(0) - (-4)(1) = 4 > 0$. The characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 + 4 = 0$, so $\lambda = \pm 2i$, purely imaginary. A zero trace with positive determinant gives a center: closed orbits around the equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For $J = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, the equilibrium is
questions.explanation: $\tau = -2 < 0$, $\Delta = (-1)(-1) - (-2)(2) = 1 + 4 = 5 > 0$, and the discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 4 - 20 = -16 < 0$. Complex eigenvalues with negative real part ($\lambda = -1 \pm 2i$) give a stable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the trace-determinant plane, the parabola $\tau^2 = 4\Delta$ separates
questions.explanation: The eigenvalues are real when the discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta \ge 0$ (nodes) and complex when $\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0$ (spirals/foci). The curve $\tau^2 = 4\Delta$ is exactly where the discriminant vanishes, so it is the node-spiral boundary.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For $J = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, the equilibrium is
questions.explanation: Eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $3$ and $2$, both real and positive. Two positive eigenvalues give an unstable node: every direction grows. (Check: $\tau = 5 > 0$, $\Delta = 6 > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 1 > 0$.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An equilibrium with eigenvalues $\lambda = 2 \pm 3i$ (positive real part, nonzero imaginary part) is
questions.explanation: Complex eigenvalues mean spiralling trajectories; the positive real part ($2 > 0$) means the spiral grows outward. So the equilibrium is an unstable spiral (unstable focus).
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Hartman-Grobman theorem justifies linear stability analysis by stating that, near a hyperbolic equilibrium (no eigenvalue with zero real part), the nonlinear flow is
options: topologically equivalent to its linearization locally
questions.explanation: Hartman-Grobman says that near a hyperbolic equilibrium the nonlinear system and its linearization are topologically conjugate: their phase portraits match up to a continuous local change of coordinates. This is why the Jacobian eigenvalues correctly predict the local behaviour, provided no eigenvalue is purely imaginary.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Linear stability analysis can fail to determine stability when
options: an eigenvalue has zero real part (a non-hyperbolic equilibrium)
questions.explanation: If an eigenvalue lies on the imaginary axis (zero real part), the equilibrium is non-hyperbolic and the linearization is marginal. Then nonlinear terms decide the actual stability, and methods such as a Lyapunov function or center manifold reduction are needed.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A population model $\dot{N} = rN(1 - N/K)$ is linearized about the carrying capacity $N^* = K$. The eigenvalue (slope) there is
questions.explanation: With $f(N) = rN(1 - N/K)$, $f'(N) = r(1 - 2N/K)$. At $N^* = K$: $f'(K) = r(1 - 2) = -r < 0$. The negative slope confirms the carrying capacity is a stable equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The system $\dot{x} = y$, $\dot{y} = -x - y$ has its only equilibrium at the origin. Its type is
questions.explanation: The Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, with $\tau = -1$, $\Delta = (0)(-1) - (1)(-1) = 1 > 0$, and $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0$. Complex eigenvalues with negative real part ($\lambda = -\tfrac{1}{2} \pm \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$) make the origin a stable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the linear system with $J = \begin{pmatrix} -4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, the equilibrium is a stable node. Trajectories approach the origin tangent to the
questions.explanation: Eigenvalues are $-4$ (fast decay, along the $x$-axis) and $-1$ (slow decay, along the $y$-axis). As $t \to \infty$ the fast mode dies first, so trajectories align with the slow eigendirection, the $y$-axis, and come in tangent to it.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A nonzero equilibrium of a predator-prey style system has Jacobian with $\tau = 0$ and $\Delta > 0$. The linear analysis predicts
questions.explanation: Zero trace with positive determinant gives purely imaginary eigenvalues $\lambda = \pm i\sqrt{\Delta}$, so the linearization is a center with closed orbits and neutral oscillations. (This is the borderline case where nonlinear terms may change the true behaviour.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Analyze $\dot{x} = x - x^2$ on the real line.
questions.solution: The equilibria solve $x(1 - x) = 0$, giving $x^* = 0$ and $x^* =$ {{1}}. With $f'(x) = 1 - 2x$, the slope $f'(0) = 1$ is positive, so $x^* = 0$ is {{2}}. The slope $f'(1) = -1$ is negative, so $x^* = 1$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Equilibria $x^* = 0, 1$. $f'(x) = 1 - 2x$: $f'(0) = 1 > 0$ unstable; $f'(1) = -1 < 0$ stable.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A planar linear system has Jacobian $J = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The trace is $\tau =$ {{1}} and the determinant is $\Delta =$ {{2}}. Since $\tau < 0$ and $\Delta > 0$ with real eigenvalues, the equilibrium is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\tau = -3 + (-1) = -4$. $\Delta = (-3)(-1) - (1)(0) = 3$. Eigenvalues are $-3$ and $-1$ (both real, negative), and $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 16 - 12 = 4 > 0$, so a stable node.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Use the trace-determinant test on $J = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 5 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The trace is $\tau =$ {{1}} and the determinant is $\Delta =$ {{2}}. With $\tau = 0$ and $\Delta > 0$, the eigenvalues are purely imaginary and the equilibrium is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\tau = 2 + (-2) = 0$. $\Delta = (2)(-2) - (-1)(5) = -4 + 5 = 1 > 0$. Characteristic equation $\lambda^2 + 1 = 0$ gives $\lambda = \pm i$, a center.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Classify the equilibrium of $J = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The determinant is $\Delta =$ {{1}}. Because the determinant is negative, the eigenvalues are real with opposite signs, so the equilibrium is a {{2}}, which is always {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\Delta = (1)(1) - (3)(3) = 1 - 9 = -8 < 0$. Negative determinant gives a saddle (eigenvalues $\lambda = 1 \pm 3 = 4, -2$ of opposite sign), and saddles are always unstable.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the scalar equation $\dot{x} = x^2 - 9$, study the two equilibria.
questions.solution: Setting $x^2 - 9 = 0$ gives $x^* = \pm 3$. With $f'(x) = 2x$, the slope at $x^* = -3$ is $f'(-3) =$ {{1}}, so this equilibrium is {{2}}. The slope at $x^* = 3$ is $f'(3) =$ {{3}}, so that equilibrium is {{4}}.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A planar system has Jacobian $J = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -5 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ at an equilibrium.
questions.solution: The trace is $\tau =$ {{1}} and the determinant is $\Delta = 6$. The discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta =$ {{2}} is negative, so the eigenvalues are complex. Since the real part of the eigenvalues equals $\tau/2 < 0$, the equilibrium is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\tau = -1 + (-1) = -2$. $\Delta = (-1)(-1) - (-5)(1) = 1 + 5 = 6$. $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 4 - 24 = -20 < 0$, complex eigenvalues $\lambda = -1 \pm i\sqrt{5}$ with negative real part, so a stable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology
